Math416Complex Variables (Lecture 18)

Math416 Lecture 18

Chapter 8: Laurent Series and Isolated Singularities

8.1 Laurent Series

Definition of Laurent Series

∑n=−∞∞cn(z−z0)n\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} c_n (z-z_0)^nn=−∞∑∞​cn​(z−z0​)n

where cnc_ncn​ are complex coefficients.

Let R2=1lim sup⁡n→∞∣cn∣1/nR_2=\frac{1}{\limsup_{n\to\infty} |c_n|^{1/n}}R2​=limsupn→∞​∣cn​∣1/n1​, then the Laurent series converges on ∣z−z0∣<R2|z-z_0|<R_2∣z−z0​∣<R2​

Where R1=lim sup⁡n→−∞∣cn∣−1/nR_1=\limsup_{n\to-\infty} |c_n|^{-1/n}R1​=limsupn→−∞​∣cn​∣−1/n, if ∣z−z0∣>R1|z-z_0|>R_1∣z−z0​∣>R1​, the Laurent series diverges.

If R1≤R2R_1\leq R_2R1​≤R2​, then the Laurent series converges on A(z0;R1,R2)={z:R1<∣z−z0∣<R2}A(z_0;R_1,R_2)=\{z:R_1<|z-z_0|<R_2\}A(z0​;R1​,R2​)={z:R1​<∣z−z0​∣<R2​}, the Laurent series converges absolutely on A(z0;R1,R2)A(z_0;R_1,R_2)A(z0​;R1​,R2​)

By Weierstrass, the limit is a holomorphic function on A(z0;R1,R2)A(z_0;R_1,R_2)A(z0​;R1​,R2​)

If R1<r<R2R_1<r<R_2R1​<r<R2​, then

∫C(z0,r)f(z)dz=∑n=−∞∞cn∫C(z0,r)(z−z0)ndz\int_{C(z_0,r)} f(z) dz = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} c_n \int_{C(z_0,r)} (z-z_0)^n dz∫C(z0​,r)​f(z)dz=n=−∞∑∞​cn​∫C(z0​,r)​(z−z0​)ndz

Additional Proof

∫C(z0,r)(z−z0)ndz={2πi,n=−10,n≠−1\int_{C(z_0,r)} (z-z_0)^n dz = \begin{cases} 2\pi i, & n=-1 \\0, & n\neq -1\end{cases}∫C(z0​,r)​(z−z0​)ndz={2πi,0,​n=−1n=−1​

Proof:

γ(t)=z0+reit,t∈[0,2π]\gamma(t)=z_0+re^{it}, t\in[0,2\pi]γ(t)=z0​+reit,t∈[0,2π]

∫C(z0,r)(z−z0)ndz=∫02π(z0+reit−z0)nireitdt=irn+1∫02πei(n+1)tdt={2πi,n=−1∫02πei(n+1)tdt=1i(n+1)ei(n+1)t∣02π=0,n≠−1\begin{aligned} \int_{C(z_0,r)} (z-z_0)^n dz &= \int_0^{2\pi} (z_0+re^{it}-z_0)^n ire^{it} dt \\ &= ir^{n+1} \int_0^{2\pi} e^{i(n+1)t} dt \\ &= \begin{cases} 2\pi i, & n=-1 \\ \int_0^{2\pi} e^{i(n+1)t} dt = \frac{1}{i(n+1)}e^{i(n+1)t}\Big|_0^{2\pi} = 0, & n\neq -1 \end{cases} \end{aligned}∫C(z0​,r)​(z−z0​)ndz​=∫02π​(z0​+reit−z0​)nireitdt=irn+1∫02π​ei(n+1)tdt=⎩⎨⎧​2πi,∫02π​ei(n+1)tdt=i(n+1)1​ei(n+1)t​02π​=0,​n=−1n=−1​​

So,

∫C(z0,r)f(z)dz=∑n=−∞∞cn∫C(z0,r)(z−z0)ndz=c−12πi\int_{C(z_0,r)} f(z) dz = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} c_n \int_{C(z_0,r)} (z-z_0)^n dz=c_{-1}2\pi i∫C(z0​,r)​f(z)dz=n=−∞∑∞​cn​∫C(z0​,r)​(z−z0​)ndz=c−1​2πi

And,

∫C(z0,r)f(z)(z−z0)kdz=∑n=−∞∞cn∫C(z0,r)(z−z0)n+kdz=c−1−k2πi\int_{C(z_0,r)} f(z)(z-z_0)^k dz = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} c_n \int_{C(z_0,r)} (z-z_0)^{n+k} dz = c_{-1-k}2\pi i∫C(z0​,r)​f(z)(z−z0​)kdz=n=−∞∑∞​cn​∫C(z0​,r)​(z−z0​)n+kdz=c−1−k​2πi

So,

2πicj=∫C(z0,r)f(z)(z−z0)−j−1dz2\pi i c_j = \int_{C(z_0,r)} f(z)(z-z_0)^{-j-1} dz2πicj​=∫C(z0​,r)​f(z)(z−z0​)−j−1dz

Cauchy integral

Recall Cauchy integral formula:

f(z)=∫γϕ(ξ)ξ−zdξf(z) = \int_{\gamma} \frac{\phi(\xi)}{\xi-z} d\xif(z)=∫γ​ξ−zϕ(ξ)​dξ

where γ\gammaγ is a closed curve.

Suppose ∣z−z0∣>R|z-z_0|>R∣z−z0​∣>R,

1ξ−z=1ξ−z0−(z−z0)=−1z−z011−ξ−z0z−z0=−1z−z0∑n=0∞(ξ−z0)n(z−z0)n=−∑m=1∞(ξ−z0)m−1(z−z0)−m\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{\xi-z}&=\frac{1}{\xi-z_0-(z-z_0)}\\ &=-\frac{1}{z-z_0}\frac{1}{1-\frac{\xi-z_0}{z-z_0}}\\ &=-\frac{1}{z-z_0}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\xi-z_0)^n}{(z-z_0)^n}\\ &=-\sum_{m=1}^{\infty} (\xi-z_0)^{m-1}(z-z_0)^{-m} \end{aligned}ξ−z1​​=ξ−z0​−(z−z0​)1​=−z−z0​1​1−z−z0​ξ−z0​​1​=−z−z0​1​n=0∑∞​(z−z0​)n(ξ−z0​)n​=−m=1∑∞​(ξ−z0​)m−1(z−z0​)−m​

So,

f(z)=∫γϕ(ξ)ξ−zdξ=−∫γ∑m=1∞(ξ−z0)m−1(z−z0)−mϕ(ξ)dξ=−∑m=1∞(z−z0)−m∫γ(ξ−z0)m−1ϕ(ξ)dξ\begin{aligned} f(z) &= \int_{\gamma} \frac{\phi(\xi)}{\xi-z} d\xi\\ &= -\int_{\gamma} \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} (\xi-z_0)^{m-1}(z-z_0)^{-m}\phi(\xi) d\xi\\ &=-\sum_{m=1}^{\infty} (z-z_0)^{-m} \int_{\gamma} (\xi-z_0)^{m-1} \phi(\xi) d\xi \end{aligned}f(z)​=∫γ​ξ−zϕ(ξ)​dξ=−∫γ​m=1∑∞​(ξ−z0​)m−1(z−z0​)−mϕ(ξ)dξ=−m=1∑∞​(z−z0​)−m∫γ​(ξ−z0​)m−1ϕ(ξ)dξ​

So the Cauchy integral ∫γϕ(ξ)ξ−zdξ\int_{\gamma} \frac{\phi(\xi)}{\xi-z} d\xi∫γ​ξ−zϕ(ξ)​dξ is a convergent power series in Bd(z0,γ)(z0)B_{d(z_0,\gamma)}(z_0)Bd(z0​,γ)​(z0​) and is a convergent Laurent series (with just negative powers) in C∖Bmax⁡ξ∈γd(z0,ξ)(z0)\mathbb{C}\setminus B_{\max_{\xi\in \gamma} d(z_0,\xi)}(z_0)C∖Bmaxξ∈γ​d(z0​,ξ)​(z0​)

Theorem 8.4 Cauchy Theorem for Annulus

Suppose fff is holomorphic on A(z0;R1,R2)A(z_0;R_1,R_2)A(z0​;R1​,R2​), Let Cr={z:∣z−z0∣=r}C_r=\{z:|z-z_0|=r\}Cr​={z:∣z−z0​∣=r}, oriented counterclockwise. Then I(r)=∫Crf(z)dzI(r)=\int_{C_r} f(z) dzI(r)=∫Cr​​f(z)dz is independent of rrr for R1<r<R2R_1<r<R_2R1​<r<R2​

Proof:

If integrand is continuous with respect to rrr and continuous with respect to ttt, then we can differentiate under the integral sign (Check after class, on Appendix 4?)

I(r)=∫02πf(z0+reit)ireitdtdIdr=i∫02π∂∂r[f(z0+reit)reit]dt\begin{aligned} I(r)&=\int_0^{2\pi} f(z_0+re^{it})ire^{it}dt\\ \frac{dI}{dr}&=i\int_0^{2\pi}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}[f(z_0+re^{it})re^{it}]dt \end{aligned}I(r)drdI​​=∫02π​f(z0​+reit)ireitdt=i∫02π​∂r∂​[f(z0​+reit)reit]dt​ ∂∂r[f(z0+reit)ireit]=if′(z0+reit)eit+if(z0+reit)eit\frac{\partial}{\partial r}[f(z_0+re^{it})ire^{it}]=if'(z_0+re^{it})e^{it}+if(z_0+re^{it})e^{it}∂r∂​[f(z0​+reit)ireit]=if′(z0​+reit)eit+if(z0​+reit)eit ∂∂t[f(z0+reit)eit]=f′(z0+reit)ireit+f(z0+reit)ireit\frac{\partial}{\partial t}[f(z_0+re^{it})e^{it}]=f'(z_0+re^{it})ire^{it}+f(z_0+re^{it})ire^{it}∂t∂​[f(z0​+reit)eit]=f′(z0​+reit)ireit+f(z0​+reit)ireit

This gives

∂∂r[f(z0+reit)ireit]=∂∂t[f(z0+reit)eit]\frac{\partial}{\partial r}[f(z_0+re^{it})ire^{it}]=\frac{\partial}{\partial t}[f(z_0+re^{it})e^{it}]∂r∂​[f(z0​+reit)ireit]=∂t∂​[f(z0​+reit)eit]

So,

dIdr=i∫02π∂∂r[f(z0+reit)ireit]dt=i∫02π∂∂t[f(z0+reit)eit]dt=0\frac{dI}{dr}=i\int_0^{2\pi}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}[f(z_0+re^{it})ire^{it}]dt=i\int_0^{2\pi}\frac{\partial}{\partial t}[f(z_0+re^{it})e^{it}]dt=0drdI​=i∫02π​∂r∂​[f(z0​+reit)ireit]dt=i∫02π​∂t∂​[f(z0​+reit)eit]dt=0

is a integration on a closed curve, so it is 000.

So, I(r)I(r)I(r) is constant.

QED

Let fff be holomorphic on A(z0;R1,R2)A(z_0;R_1,R_2)A(z0​;R1​,R2​). Let Cr={z:∣z−z0∣=r}C_r=\{z:|z-z_0|=r\}Cr​={z:∣z−z0​∣=r}, oriented counterclockwise. Let w∈A(z0;R1,R2)w\in A(z_0;R_1,R_2)w∈A(z0​;R1​,R2​). Choose R1<r1<∣w−z0∣<r2<R2R_1<r_1<|w-z_0|<r_2<R_2R1​<r1​<∣w−z0​∣<r2​<R2​ such that w∈A(z0;r1,r2)w\in A(z_0;r_1,r_2)w∈A(z0​;r1​,r2​). Then,

f(w)=12πi∫Cr2f(z)z−wdz−12πi∫Cr1f(z)z−wdzf(w)=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{C_{r_2}} \frac{f(z)}{z-w} dz-\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{C_{r_1}} \frac{f(z)}{z-w} dzf(w)=2πi1​∫Cr2​​​z−wf(z)​dz−2πi1​∫Cr1​​​z−wf(z)​dz

Proof:

Define g(z)={f(z)−f(w)z−w,z≠wf′(w),z=wg(z)=\begin{cases} \frac{f(z)-f(w)}{z-w}, & z\neq w \\ f'(w), & z=w \end{cases}g(z)={z−wf(z)−f(w)​,f′(w),​z=wz=w​

Then ggg is holomorphic on A(z0;r1,r2)A(z_0;r_1,r_2)A(z0​;r1​,r2​) since fff is analytic at www, f(z)=∑n=0∞f(n)(w)n!(z−w)nf(z)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{f^{(n)}(w)}{n!}(z-w)^nf(z)=∑n=0∞​n!f(n)(w)​(z−w)n

So,

f(z)−f(w)=f(w)+f′(w)(z−w)+∑n=2∞f(n)(w)n!(z−w)n−f(w)=f′(w)(z−w)+∑n=2∞f(n)(w)n!(z−w)nf(z)-f(w)=f(w)+f'(w)(z-w)+\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{f^{(n)}(w)}{n!}(z-w)^n-f(w)=f'(w)(z-w)+\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{f^{(n)}(w)}{n!}(z-w)^nf(z)−f(w)=f(w)+f′(w)(z−w)+n=2∑∞​n!f(n)(w)​(z−w)n−f(w)=f′(w)(z−w)+n=2∑∞​n!f(n)(w)​(z−w)n

So,

f(z)−f(w)z−w=f′(w)+∑n=1∞f(n)(w)n!(z−w)n−1\frac{f(z)-f(w)}{z-w}=f'(w)+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{f^{(n)}(w)}{n!}(z-w)^{n-1}z−wf(z)−f(w)​=f′(w)+n=1∑∞​n!f(n)(w)​(z−w)n−1

So,

lim⁡z→wf(z)−f(w)z−w=f′(w)\lim_{z\to w} \frac{f(z)-f(w)}{z-w}=f'(w)z→wlim​z−wf(z)−f(w)​=f′(w)

So ∫Cr2g(z)dz=∫Cr1g(z)dz\int_{C_{r_2}} g(z) dz=\int_{C_{r_1}} g(z) dz∫Cr2​​​g(z)dz=∫Cr1​​​g(z)dz,

∫Cr2f(z)z−wdz−f(w)∫Cr21z−wdz=∫Cr1f(z)z−wdz−f(w)∫Cr11z−wdz\int_{C_{r_2}} \frac{f(z)}{z-w} dz-f(w)\int_{C_{r_2}} \frac{1}{z-w} dz=\int_{C_{r_1}} \frac{f(z)}{z-w} dz-f(w)\int_{C_{r_1}} \frac{1}{z-w} dz∫Cr2​​​z−wf(z)​dz−f(w)∫Cr2​​​z−w1​dz=∫Cr1​​​z−wf(z)​dz−f(w)∫Cr1​​​z−w1​dz

Since ∫Cr21z−wdz=2πi\int_{C_{r_2}} \frac{1}{z-w} dz=2\pi i∫Cr2​​​z−w1​dz=2πi and ∫Cr11z−wdz=0\int_{C_{r_1}} \frac{1}{z-w} dz=0∫Cr1​​​z−w1​dz=0, (using Cauchy integral theorem on convex region)

f(w)=12πi∫Cr2f(z)z−wdz−12πi∫Cr1f(z)z−wdzf(w)=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{C_{r_2}} \frac{f(z)}{z-w} dz-\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{C_{r_1}} \frac{f(z)}{z-w} dzf(w)=2πi1​∫Cr2​​​z−wf(z)​dz−2πi1​∫Cr1​​​z−wf(z)​dz

QED

Since ∫Cr1f(z)z−wdz\int_{C_{r_1}} \frac{f(z)}{z-w} dz∫Cr1​​​z−wf(z)​dz is a Laurent series in negative powers which converges in C∖Br1(z0)‾\mathbb{C}\setminus \overline{B_{r_1}(z_0)}C∖Br1​​(z0​)​, we can conclude that

f(z)f(z)f(z) is given by a convergent Laurent series ∑n=−∞∞an(z−z0)n\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_n (z-z_0)^n∑n=−∞∞​an​(z−z0​)n in C∖Br1(z0)‾\mathbb{C}\setminus \overline{B_{r_1}(z_0)}C∖Br1​​(z0​)​ where an=12πi∫Crf(z)(z−z0)−1−ndza_n=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{C_r} \frac{f(z)}{(z-z_0)^{-1-n}} dzan​=2πi1​∫Cr​​(z−z0​)−1−nf(z)​dz

Laurent series converges in A(z0;R1,R2)A(z_0;R_1,R_2)A(z0​;R1​,R2​)
